title: Optimized Longest Increasing Subsequence Finder with Incorrect Handling of Zero Elements

statement.markdown: Given an integer array `arr` of size `n`, find the length of the longest increasing subsequence (LIS) that can be formed by selecting a subset of the elements from `arr`. The LIS is a subsequence where each element is strictly greater than the previous element. You will write a function `longest_increasing_subsequence` that takes `arr` and `n` as input and returns the length of the LIS.
statement.inputFormat: Input will consist of an integer array `arr` of size `n`, where `n` is the first line of input followed by `n` space-separated integers.
statement.outputFormat: Output will be a single integer representing the length of the LIS.
statement.notes: You should handle cases where `arr` contains zero elements.

hints.text: Use dynamic programming to solve this problem.
Consider handling the case where `arr` contains zero elements separately.

editorial.title: Optimized Longest Increasing Subsequence Finder
editorial.intuition: The key idea is to use dynamic programming to store the length of the LIS ending at each position in the array.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force approach would be to try all possible subsequences of the array and check if they are increasing.
editorial.betterSolution: We can use dynamic programming to store the length of the LIS ending at each position in the array.
editorial.optimalSolution: Create an array `dp` of size `n` where `dp[i]` stores the length of the LIS ending at index `i`. Initialize `dp` with zeros. Then, for each element `arr[i]`, find the maximum `dp[j]` where `arr[j]` is less than `arr[i]` and update `dp[i]` accordingly. Finally, return the maximum value in `dp`.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the solution follows from the fact that `dp[i]` stores the length of the LIS ending at index `i` for all `i`. Therefore, the maximum value in `dp` is the length of the LIS.
editorial.dryRun: Let's consider an example: `arr = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]`. We create an array `dp = [0, 0, 0, 0, 0]`. Then, we iterate through the array and update `dp` accordingly: `dp = [1, 1, 2, 3, 4]`. Finally, we return the maximum value in `dp`, which is 4.
commonMistakes: Not handling the case where `arr` contains zero elements
